ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori prawns; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for tandoori-marinated grilled prawns.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 covers prawn as a food substance; no specific concept for tandoori preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of tandoori prawns is 2 large tiger prawns (approximately 120 g), typically served with mint chutney and sliced onion as a starter.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — adapted with tandoori marinade adjustment (yogurt, cream, spices, oil)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods / SR Legacy; marinade contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base shrimp values derived from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180, cooked shrimp). Marinade contribution (yogurt, cream, oil, spices) estimated using ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for tandoori marinade components. Final per-100g values represent the composite cooked dish including marinade absorption, consistent with published tandoori prawn analyses in Indian food composition literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — seafood and protein-dominant dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori prawns. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Protein-dominant foods with minimal starch content consistently yield GI values in the 10–20 range. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as (15 × 3.2 × 1.20) / 100 = 0.58, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~624 mg per 120 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2 pieces and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments, as the tandoori marinade and spice blend contribute approximately 624 mg sodium per serving, representing roughly 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori prawns are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and minimal carbohydrate content of 3.2 g per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of only 0.6 per standard serving; the high protein content (18.5 g per 100 g) further supports postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tandoori prawns provide beneficial lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol content (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium load; consumption of 2 pieces as an occasional starter is acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern, but frequent large servings should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Prawns (Tandoori Jheenga) is a classic North Indian seafood dish in which large tiger prawns are marinated in a spiced yogurt and cream mixture — typically incorporating tandoori masala, ginger-garlic paste, red chilli, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and lemon juice — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char and caramelised crust while keeping the prawn flesh juicy and tender inside. The dish is served hot, often garnished with sliced onion, lemon wedges, and fresh mint chutney. Nutritionally, tandoori prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 18.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making them highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-GI dietary patterns. The moderate sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. In Singapore, tandoori prawns are served as a starter or sharing plate at North Indian restaurants, typically priced at SGD 16–20 for 2–4 pieces. The Gulzaar variant at Bombay Curry And Kebabs on South Bridge Road is noted for its creamy, spicy marinade profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawns?
Is tandoori prawns good for diabetics?
Is tandoori prawns high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for tandoori prawns exists in a single approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180) with marinade contribution estimated using ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. The composite estimate is internally consistent and consistent with published Indian food composition literature, but has not been verified against a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked shrimp and is not available in the ICMR-NIN marinade component data. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as neither shrimp nor the standard tandoori marinade ingredients contribute industrial trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Serving size set at 120 g representing 2 large tiger prawns with marinade, consistent with the field survey listing of 2 pieces as the base serving. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database applied to high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood; no direct GI measurement for tandoori prawns was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 3.2 × 1.20) / 100 = 0.58, rounded to 0.6. The food is classified as North Indian cuisine served in a Singapore restaurant context; multilingual names are provided in English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's North Indian Muslim cuisine profile; kosher status is not confirmed. Allergen list includes crustaceans (primary), dairy (yogurt and cream in marinade), and mustard (present in some tandoori masala blends).
